details.reasoning: Technical 1/4, sourceFaith 2/3, logic 2/3. The source rumor is represented broadly correctly: Dan McAteer did post that Sonnet 5 "may come" with an "Agentic Swarm" and said "Looks like tomorrow is the day." But the post overstates that rumor in several places: Anthropic’s official February 2026 releases were Claude Opus 4.6 on February 5 and Claude Sonnet 4.6 on February 17, not a public "Claude Sonnet 5," so the title/frame leans on a model name that was not publicly released; it also upgrades a speculative leak into claims like the direction being "basically certain" and treats Dan’s track record as fact without evidence. The overall argument is coherent, but major conclusions about an industry shift are drawn from a single unverified post.

Technical 3/4, sourceFaithfulness 2/3, logic 2/3. The core story matches widely reproduced reports of Jaana Dogan's Jan. 2, 2026 X post: Claude Code produced a toy, non-production implementation of a distributed agent orchestrator in about an hour, and Dogan said the prompt contained no proprietary details. The post loses some source nuance by escalating this into a headline about fully recreating 'a year's heart and soul,' and it includes several reaction metrics and third-party claims (5.4M views, Gene Sobolev, Thomas Power) without first-hand sourcing in the post, so those are at best unverifiable as written.

Technical 3/4, sourceFaithfulness 2/3, logic 3/3. The core translation tracks Karpathy's article well on RLVR, jagged intelligence, Cursor, Claude Code, vibe coding, and Nano Banana, and the argument remains internally coherent. Issues: the frontmatter `originalDate` is wrong because the source page is dated 2025-12-19, not 2026-01-15; the Claude Code section overstates the source into a simpler 'runs locally' claim while Karpathy explicitly says the key distinction is not strictly local vs. cloud but access to the already-booted computer, data, secrets, config, and low latency; and some vivid specifics in the vibe-coding and visual-interaction sections (for example the 5-minute app example and 'human brain has 30% devoted to vision') are not in the source and are presented more definitively than supported.

Technical 3/4, sourceFaithfulness 1/3, logic 2/3. Core facts from Karpathy's Jan 26, 2026 X post are mostly right: the 80/20 workflow flip, the 'hurts the ego' point, 'watch them like a hawk,' 'slopacolypse,' and the December 2025 'threshold of coherence' all match the source. But the post overstates several source claims: it turns Karpathy's tentative question that the 10x-engineer gap 'quite possibly' grows into a firm prediction with added causal explanation, adds examples like AI writing full docs / learning a new stack that are not in the source thread, and renders 'slightly sloppy, hasty junior dev' as a more embellished 'careless, impatient, but very knowledgeable junior developer,' which changes nuance.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaith 2/3, logic 2/3. The core translation of Karpathy's tweet is broadly faithful to the mirrored text, but the post adds unsupported or incorrect factual detail around it: the claim that "2025 下半年" is when Claude Code and MCP appeared is wrong because Anthropic introduced MCP on 2024-11-25 and Claude Code on 2025-02-24, and the post gives hard engagement numbers and commenter attributions without a first-hand source. The argument is mostly coherent, but several conclusions lean on uncited social-media reactions and overstated timeline claims rather than evidence from the source post itself.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaithfulness 1/3, logic 3/3. The post is mostly aligned with Raschka’s themes, but it introduces several factual or source-faithfulness problems: the frontmatter `originalDate` is wrong (the source page is dated December 30, 2025, not 2026-01-15), it misstates Raschka’s Lightning AI role as something like a chief researcher, it overstates RLVR as eliminating the need for human labeling even though the source says SFT/RLHF remain important, and it turns the DeepSeek V3 cost discussion into a definitive "$5M" claim while dropping Raschka’s caveats. The biggest concrete error is timing: the translation says math-olympiad gold-level performance was achieved in early 2025, but Raschka’s source references DeepSeekMath-V2 as coming out only 'last month' relative to December 30, 2025, so that achievement was late 2025, not early 2025.

Technical accuracy 2/4, sourceFaithfulness 2/3, logic 2/3. The core source tweet is represented reasonably well, but the post introduces factual overstatements: it says Rauch wrote in the past tense even though the tweet uses present-tense verbs in a future framing, and it presents DHH as having said on Lex Fridman that "AI 根本不會寫程式," which I could not verify in the transcript and which overstates his more nuanced comments about current AI coding limits. The rest is mostly interpretation, but several conclusions about Rauch's intent and the inevitability/marketing significance of the tweet are speculative rather than source-grounded.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaith 2/3, logic 2/3. The core Sholto Douglas quotes about 2026, continual learning, knowledge work, home robots, and software engineering going "utterly wild" are consistent with secondary coverage of the No Priors discussion, but the post makes a major unsupported jump when it says Google DeepMind researchers at NeurIPS 2025 predicted 2026 as the turning point and developed a "nested method" for LLM context: the traceable NeurIPS paper is "Nested Learning," by Google Research authors, and its abstract says it shows "promising results," not that continual learning is "fully realized." The domino-chain framing also overstates causality by presenting continual learning as making later predictions suddenly "reasonable," which is speculation rather than source-backed fact.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaithfulness 1/3, logic 2/3. The core quote from Simon is real and the high-level idea of agentic loops matches his linked post, but this piece attributes a lot more to the X post than the cited source actually contains, adds an unsupported quantitative claim ("每秒可以嘗試幾千次"), and makes an overbroad historical claim that earlier AI coding tools "根本跑不了迴圈" even though agentic/iterative coding tools and GitHub Copilot agent/CLI capabilities existed by 2025. The article is mostly coherent, but several conclusions are author extrapolations presented as if they directly came from Simon’s post/tweet.

Technical 3/4, sourceFaithfulness 1/3, logic 2/3. The core idea is supported: Alex Albert did post about suppressing the "not worth my time" instinct and the screenshot-renaming example, and Simon Willison is a Django co-creator. But the post's main Simon quote appears to be an embellished synthesis rather than a faithful translation of the cited source: mirrored text for Simon's linked post reads closer to "we have so many ideas... all our hard-won intuitions about that are now just wrong," not the article's much more specific wording about "25 years," "build and maintain," and "throwing it at Claude Code"; the article also states broader conclusions such as coding no longer being the bottleneck more definitively than the source supports.

Technical 3/4, sourceFaith 1/3, logic 2/3. The core agent-components discussion is broadly consistent with swyx's September 2025 framing, and the Simon Willison quote about LLMs being inherently gullible is grounded in reporting, but the post appears to misattribute Simon Willison's later definition and his 'significant piece of personal development' remark to swyx. It also drops a key source caveat: swyx's post explicitly said the minimalist definition makes people forget planning, memory, trust/auth, and evals, but this translation expands the list into seven components while largely omitting evals from the main body, which changes the emphasis of the source.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaithfulness 2/3, logic 3/3. The core results match Vercel's Jan 27, 2026 blog post: 53% baseline, 53% default skill, 79% with explicit instructions, and 100% for an 8KB AGENTS.md docs index. The main factual problem is that the post turns Vercel's qualitative wording comparison into a quantitative claim: it says 'You MUST invoke the skill' performed worse and that wording changes caused a 26-point swing, but the source only says that wording materially changed behavior and that the 'explore first' instruction reached 79%; it does not provide a separate pass rate for the 'You MUST invoke the skill' variant.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaith 1/3, logic 2/3. The core idea that Claude Code wrappers may become a major shift is plausible, but several concrete claims are inaccurate or unsupported: the post treats Anthropic's API-only Tool Search as if it were a Claude Code feature, overstates Claude Code as simply stuffing entire projects/docs into one context window, and links to "Claude Sonnet 5" even though Anthropic's public model docs list Sonnet 4.x/3.7 rather than an official Sonnet 5 release. Source faithfulness is weak because much of the article's substance (Tool Search explanation, Paolo's BlackHatWorld/marketing demo details, and the OpenCode crackdown interpretation) is not traceable to the cited X post and is presented more definitively than the available evidence supports.

technical 3/4, sourceFaith 2/3, logic 2/3. Core facts match the SiliconANGLE source: Airrived raised $6.1M, launched an "Agentic OS," and cited deployments at a Fortune 150 insurer, a global bank, a telecom infrastructure company, and a large U.S. fast-casual chain. The main issues are overstatement and nuance loss: the post turns the source's company framing about enterprise AI "summarizing" into broader factual claims about "2026 年大部分企業 AI," adds "不能推理" where the source emphasized decision/action limits, and mistranslates "fast-casual restaurant chain" into a more generic/possibly misleading "速食連鎖店" with hamburger speculation.

Technical 3/4, sourceFaith 1/3, logic 2/3. The post broadly matches the paper's themes and correctly cites the Science publication date (January 22, 2026), but it repeatedly turns conditional/speculative claims into present-tense facts: the paper explicitly says it distinguishes documented trends from projections and that many harms 'may' materialize. The main factual issue is loss of hedging and caveats, especially around swarm coordination, automated A/B testing, mass harassment, and the closing claim that this is already 'not predicting the future' but a present reality.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaithfulness 1/3, logic 2/3. The core facts about the DeepLearning.AI course are broadly right: there is a Claude Code course built with Anthropic and taught by Elie Schoppik, and Andrew Ng publicly called it the “definitive course on Claude Code.” But the post adds unsupported or misleading technical claims not present in the source, including a named Claude Code capability called “speculative branching,” a too-strong description of fully autonomous PR/test workflows despite Anthropic documenting explicit approval controls, and an exact model name like “GPT-5.2-Codex-High” that does not match OpenAI’s public naming. Faithfulness is weak because large sections (Karpathy’s workflow shift, the ‘real stack’ anecdote, Cursor/Max 5x/model usage details) are not in Andrew Ng’s post and are presented as factual context without sourcing.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaithfulness 1/3, logic 2/3. The post gets the broad news right that Anthropic positioned Claude as ad-free and used a Super Bowl campaign to contrast with OpenAI, but it overstates key facts: as of February 4, 2026 OpenAI had announced ad testing for the coming weeks and only began showing ads on February 9, and Anthropic's own post did not make an unconditional 'forever' pledge because it explicitly said it may revisit the approach later. It also injects unsupported or inflated specifics such as OpenAI infrastructure investment 'exceeding one trillion dollars' and frames Anthropic's stance as an irreversible promise, which is not faithful to the source's caveated wording.

Technical 3/4, sourceFaithfulness 2/3, logic 3/3. The post is broadly accurate about the paper's main result that longer reasoning/action sequences increase error incoherence and that the paper frames failures as more like industrial accidents than classic coherent misalignment. The main issue is nuance loss around Finding 2: the source says the relationship between intelligence and incoherence is inconsistent and task-dependent, while the post headline and summary compress this into a stronger-sounding 'smarter models are usually more of a hot mess,' which is somewhat misleading even though the body partially restores the caveat.

Technical 2/4, sourceFaith 1/3, logic 2/3. The core news is real: Apple announced Xcode 26.3 RC on February 3, 2026 with Claude Agent, Codex, and MCP support. But the post contains several factual errors or unsupported specifics: it calls this a Claude Agent SDK integration even though Apple/press materials describe Claude Agent in Xcode, says MCP was launched in 2025 even though Anthropic introduced it on November 25, 2024, claims Claude Code had over $1B in monthly revenue when public Anthropic figures refer to run-rate revenue, and adds unsourced claims about an "AI-first Xcode" WWDC 2025 plan, required Claude Pro/Team subscriptions, and a mid-February final release that was actually February 26, 2026.

Technical 3/4, sourceFaith 2/3, logic 3/3. The core story matches Boris Cherny's January 12, 2026 post and Anthropic's Cowork launch materials: users were using Claude Code for non-coding tasks, Cowork was introduced as a simpler interface for that broader work, and official materials describe Cowork as built on the same foundations with Opus 4.5-era agentic behavior. The main issues are source-faithfulness and a bit of overreach: the article adds claims like Cowork being built in under two weeks with Claude Code writing almost all the code, and a specific MCP/custom-skill explanation for oven control; these are plausible and supported by other later reports/docs, but they are not established by the cited sourceUrl itself.
